content.full_text:
County of Santa Clara
Office of the County Counsel

County Government Center, East Wing
70 West Hedding Street
San Jose, California 95110-1770
(408) 290-2111
(408) 292-7240 (FAX)

Steven M. Woodside
County Counsel

Jeanette Turvill
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

October 6, 1992

Re: Santa Clara County Transportation Agency Newsletter on
"Measure A"

Dear Ms. Turvill:

On behalf of the Santa Clara County Transit District, I
request advice as to whether the attached article on "Measure A,"
which appeared in Transit Newsletters, is affected by any of the
laws within your jurisdiction.

In June, 1992, this office gave written advice to the
Transportation Agency which described the principles contained in
caselaw (primarily Stanson v. Mott (1976) 17 Cal.3d 206), and
concluded:

"Agency staff may not "take sides" in the election
or use public funds or authority to advocate passage
or defeat of the tax measure. However, Agency staff
may provide information relevant to the tax measure
to anyone as long as the information constitutes a
fair and balanced representation of the facts, and
avoids advocating either the enactment or the defeat
of the tax measure. For example, if any group
advocating or opposing the tax requests staff
attendance at a group meeting, that attendance must
be limited to providing information, and staff must
avoid advocating passage or defeat of the measure.

Of course, individual County or District employees
may -- on their own time, at their own expense, and
acting in their own capacity as private citizens and not
under color of County or District authority --
exercise their right to participate in the electoral
process as they see fit." (June 29, 1992 memorandum
from William Anderson to Larry Reuter)

Chief Assistant County Counsel: Ann Miller Ravel
Chief Deputies: Robert J. Menilec, Susan G. Levenberg, William I. Anderson

Ltr./FPPC
October 6, 1992
Page Two


     In mid-August, this office reviewed a draft of the "Measure A
Transportation Initiative Explained" article prior to its
publication. The office concluded that the information describing
the measure, its purposes and the membership on the Transportation
Authority, was a neutral and objective description of the measure.
Since Measure A would affect the District and its ridership, we
advised orally that it would be permissible to include the Measure
A description in Agency newsletters.

     At the time the article was in draft form, the office had
before it the last two paragraphs of the article which describe
the proponents of the measure as well as listing their address and
telephone number. Readers are referred to the proponents "if you
would like more information on Measure A". Although this kind of
referral does not advocate a "yes" vote, it can be viewed as
one-sided because it features only the proponents. We were of the
view then, however, that since there was no known organized
opposition to the measure, it would be appropriate to list the
name and address of the proponents. Our views has changed because
it is now clear that there is opposition. It is therefore no
longer appropriate to continue to disseminate information which
makes no reference to the organized opposition.

     I understand that your office does not give advice on matters
covered outside the Fair Political Practices Act. However, there
may be issues covered by the Act, such as whether the cost of the
newsletter is a reportable contribution, upon which your office
does advise.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        STEVEN M. WOODSIDE
                                        County Counsel


SMW:lw
CCADM:134:p8


[illegible]

SEPTEMBER 1992

RAIL PROJECTS
& Santa Clara County Transportation Agency

Measure A Transportation Initiative Explained

You've probably heard about the
new Measure A, the transportation
initiative (known as the Santa Clara
County Local Transportation Authority
and Improvement Act) that will be on the
ballot this fall.

Measure A, a county-wide, half-cent
sales tax approved in November 1994 to
build Highway 85, widen Highway 101
and convert Route 237 into a freeway, is
due to expire March 31, 1995.

The measure on the November ballot
would not increase the current tax, but
would rather replace the half-cent sales
tax cut in place by the original 1984
measure. It passed, the new Measure A
would begin April 1, 1995. In addition to
some highway and County Expressway
improvements, revenues would primarily
be earmarked for rail transit expansion
and the operation of those expanded rail

transit lines.
• Funds will also be used to pay for
improvements necessary to enable the
existing transit system to comply with
requirements of the Americans with
Disabilities Act as well as provide funds to
acquire and operate thirty new Super
Express buses. Should Measure A pass,
the new Local Transportation Authority will
administer the revenues and oversee
expansion of transit lines.

The proposal for the sales tax extension
is headed by the Citizens Coalition for
Traffic Relief, a diverse group of business,
environmental, labor and health leaders
and local organizations.

If you would like more information on
Measure A, contact the Citizens Coalition
for Traffic Relief, 5201 Great America
Parkway, Suite 429, San Jose, CA
(408) 562-5757.

Measure A Initiative
Contains Three Elements
Rail; Express Bus; and Roads

Projects would include:
• Building a new rail extension in
Santa Clara County to connect with
BART in Alameda County;
• Increasing CalTrain service from
Gilroy to San Francisco;
• Constructing six new rail and light
rail lines to develop a comprehensive commuter rail system through-
out the county;
• Upgrading Route 87, the Guadalupe
Parkway, though downtown San
Jose, to a full freeway;
• Completing work on key inter-
changes and overloff ramps through
out the county, including:
the interchanges at Highways 85
and 87; Highways 85 and 101; and,
Highways 237 and 880;
• Synchronizing traffic signals on all
major expressways in the county;
• Implementing "Super Express" bus
service to take commuters to major
employment centers;
• Improving funding and transit
service for senior citizens, and
those with disabilities. □

Local Transportation Authority Committee Members:

Palo Alto Mayor - Gary Fazzino, Chair
San Jose Mayor - Susan Hammer, Vice Chair
Santa Clara County Supervisor - Rod Diridon
San Jose City Councilmember - Shirley Lewis
Cupertino City Councilmember - Nick Szabo

92/06-1058 7000

This also appeared in the September - October "Rider News" and "Employee Newsletter"

sections.question: On behalf of the Santa Clara County Transit District, I request advice as to whether the attached article on "Measure A," which appeared in Transit Newsletters, is affected by any of the laws within your jurisdiction. Specifically, whether the cost of the newsletter is a reportable contribution and whether the newsletter's content, which lists only proponents of Measure A with their contact information, constitutes permissible information dissemination under the Fair Political Practices Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Does the Transit District's newsletter article on Measure A, which describes the measure neutrally but lists only the proponents' contact information for more details, comply with the Fair Political Practices Act? Is the newsletter cost a reportable contribution?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from County Counsel to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. No CONCLUSION section exists. The letter references prior internal County Counsel advice from June 1992 but seeks external FPPC guidance. The document includes the actual newsletter article as an attachment showing the 'Measure A Initiative' content and proponents' contact information.

Specifically, whether the cost of the newsletter is a reportable contribution and whether the newsletter's content, which lists only proponents of Measure A with their contact information, constitutes permissible information dissemination under the Fair Political Practices Act.

embedding.summary: Santa Clara County Counsel Steven Woodside requests FPPC advice regarding whether a Transit District newsletter article on a ballot measure (Measure A) complies with campaign finance laws, particularly concerning whether the article's inclusion of only proponents' contact information (with no opposition listed) and the newsletter's cost raises reportable contribution issues.
